content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES
CASE, SCHROEDER, KNOWLSON, MOBLEY & BURNETT
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING PROFESSIONAL CORPORATIONS
JAMBOREE CENTER
DANIEL A. CASE*
5 PARK PLAZA, SUITE 800
LOS ANGELES
ARTHUR B. KNOWLSON*
IRVINE, CALIFORNIA 92714
(900 AVENUE OF THE STARS, SUITE 1900
MICHAEL J. SCHROEDER†
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90067
MICHAEL W. BURNETT*
TELEPHONE (714) 955-1050
TELEPHONE (213) 201-7581
GARY S. MOBLEY*
TELECOPIER (714) 851-9112
TELECOPIER (213) 277-6342
BARRY A. YOCH
RICK A. CIGEL
MICHAEL F. WRIGHT
STEPHEN F. DIAL
ELIZABETH PETERSON HALL
STERLING A. SMITH
TRACY L. DUHER
MARK A. WUERBEN

*A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION
†ALSO MEMBER OF HAWAII BAR

TO HALLARIN-
PLEASE RESPOND.
THANKS.
J. LARSON
8-6-90

John Larson, Esq.
Chairman of Fair Political
Practices Commission
428 J Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Abuse of Campaign Contributor List

Dear Mr. Larson:

     Attached please find a letter myself and two hundred other contributors of U.S. Congressman, Dana Rohrbacher, received. An organization entitled the so-called "National Campaign for Freedom of Expression" directly stated that they were expressly going to major campaign donors of U.S. Congressman Dana Rohrbacher to object to a particular political issue.

     It seems to me that the federal law expressly prohibits the use of this campaign list for this type of solicitation.

     It is clear that the co-called "National Campaign for Freedom Expression" is not hiding the fact that they are using a political donor's list which is required under the United States Code, to list all campaign donors for their own purposes, including potential fund raising.

     Please investigate the matter to see which laws, if any, were violated. We appreciate hearing from you in the very near future.

                                                Sincerely yours,

                                                [signature]

                                                Michael J. Schroeder

MJS/dw.782
enclosure

Coalition for Freedom of Expression
Long Beach / Orange County Chapter

June 24, 1990

Shawn Steel
610 South Harvard Boulevard
Suite 200
Los Angeles, CA 90005

Dear Mr. Steel,

On behalf of the Long Beach/Orange County Chapter of the
Coalition for Freedom of Expression, we are writing with troubling
news about the very damaging attack Representative Dana
Rohrabacher (R-Lomita) is leading against the arts and artistic
freedom in America.

Our Chapter of the National Campaign for Freedom of Expression is
made up of concerned citizens, community leaders, educators,
artists and arts professionals who live within Rep. Rohrabacher's
Congressional District. While the entire nation stands to lose if
Rohrabacher's anti-freedom and anti-art initiatives are enacted,
we feel that it is our special responsibility to lead the fight
for freedom and for the arts within the communities he was elected
to represent.

We know that you have been a significant campaign contributor to
Rep. Rohrabacher, and respect your right to support the candidate
of your choice. But, it has come to our attention that many of
Congressman Rohrabacher's supporters may not be fully aware of his
dangerous position on the issue of artistic freedom and
censorship, nor the fatal implications of legislation he sponsors
regarding the arts and the substance and spirit of the First
Amendment.

At a time when democracy and freedom are breaking out all over the
world, it is sadly ironic that in "the land of the free," Rep.
Rohrabacher and others are pushing the U.S. Congress to the brink
of adopting chilling restrictions on artistic freedom. In many
eastern-bloc countries, it was the artists who led the democratic
revolutions while in America, Senator Jesse Helms and Congressman
Rohrabacher lead the fight against artistic freedom. They are
demanding that artists and arts organizations submit to prior
restraint of free speech--forcing them to sign "loyalty oaths" not
to make or present work that might offend someone--in order to
receive Federal funding.

Helms and Rohrabacher claim that the issue is sponsorship, not
censorship, and this may seem persuasive on casual consideration.

But sponsorship is being used here to enforce censorship because
it is predicated upon the restraint of free speech. As Chief
Justice William Rehnquist wrote for the Supreme Court in a
unanimous 1983 opinion, neither by penalty nor subsidy may the
government "aim at the suppression of dangerous ideas."

Not only is artistic freedom in dire jeopardy, but worse still,
Rohrbacher and some of his colleagues are pressing for outright
elimination of the National Endowment for the Arts. The NEA is
one of the great success stories in America. It has completely
transformed public accessibility to the arts while generating
controversy in only a minute handful of the over 80,000 projects
it has funded in 25 years. And it costs each citizen only about
60 cents per year, the lowest arts contribution by any
industrialized democracy, and a very minor part of the national
budget.

That any serious consideration is being given to eliminating an
agency with such a track record of excellence and responsibility
attests to the distorted case being manufactured by its
detractors. That Representative Rohrbacher and his allies have
inflated this case to the point where it preoccupies Congress,
while such expensive and pressing concerns such as the budget
deficit, crime, drugs, homeless Americans, and the environment cry
out for Congressional attention, makes us wonder where our
Representative's priorities lie.

The founders of our nation were guided by a visionary commitment
to diversity. The suppression of diversity, the censorship of
artistic expression, is one of the hallmarks of an undemocratic
society. It is something that Americans have been proud to
denounce in other governments. All free societies were outraged
when Iran condemned Salman Rushdie for his novel The Satanic
Verses. All free societies cheered when the great Czech
playwright Vaclav Havel, once censored by his government, became
president of his newly democratized country.

As members of the greatest free society in the world, and the
model for the new democracies of the world, we must lead the fight
against censorship and the suppression of free expression. The
National Endowment for the Arts must be encouraged to support a
wide range of artists and arts spaces. It must not be crippled by
restrictions or censorship, or suffer elimination.

Please contact Rep. Rohrbacher and ask him to reconsider his
dangerously misguided position.

For the Membership of the Long Beach/Orange County Chapter of the
Coalition for Freedom of Expression, I am

Sincerely yours,

Bill Viola

Coalition for Freedom of Expression
Long Beach / Orange County Chapter


June 24, 1990


Shawn Steel
610 South Harvard Boulevard
Suite 200
Los Angeles, CA  90005

Please contact Rep. Rohrabacher and ask him to reconsider his

For the Membership of the Long Beach/Orange County Chapter of the
Coalition for Freedom of Expression, I am

Sincerely yours,

Bill Viola

sections.question_synthetic: Whether the use of a campaign contributor list by the Coalition for Freedom of Expression to solicit major donors of U.S. Congressman Dana Rohrabacher regarding a political issue violates federal or California law.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming complaint/request letter to FPPC, not an FPPC response. No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections present. Document includes the complaint letter from attorney Schroeder and two copies of the Coalition's solicitation letter to campaign contributors. Confidence reduced due to unclear document boundaries and lack of formal FPPC structure.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Whether the use of a campaign contributor list by the Coalition for Freedom of Expression to solicit major donors of U.S. Congressman Dana Rohrabacher regarding a political issue violates federal or California law.

embedding.summary: Michael J. Schroeder writes to FPPC Chairman John Larson on behalf of 200+ contributors to Congressman Dana Rohrabacher, alleging that the Coalition for Freedom of Expression improperly used Rohrabacher's campaign donor list to solicit these contributors regarding the Representative's position on artistic freedom and the National Endowment for the Arts, and requesting investigation of potential federal law violations.
